figures.caption: Fig. 3: The Illustration of state-based modeling of action flow matching: It reformulates the action modeling from predicting actions as absolute values to predicting actions as updates to a feature-space state accumulated by prior actions. Extended formulation and architectural adjustment is adopted for larger scaled VLA model and mainstream benchmarks.

Table 1: Main results on the LIBERO benchmark. We present the performance and efficiency of StreamingVLA and baseline methods. The Taction and Thalt are adopted to measure the speed and fluency of execution. The “AFM” and “AEO” represent the “action flow matching” and “adaptive early observation” technique. The “NEO” stands for the “naive early observation”. The “ANAO” stands for the “action norm based adaptive observation” as mentioned in Sec. 4.2

Fig. 1: StreamingVLA explores streaming execution of VLA models by enabling different stage to be runned in a asynchronous manner. By overlapping the latency for stages, without sacrificing performance, it achieves 2.4× end-to-end speedup, and 6.5 × execution halting time reduction, enabling fast and fluent execution.

Fig. 7: Architecture of the predictor for adaptive early observation. During training, the model receives the early-frame embedding and the pending action sequence as conditions and predicts a residual embedding ∆embedding that estimates the latent change toward the future frame. The model is trained using an MSE loss between the ground-truth late-frame embedding and the reconstructed representation (embeddingearly+∆embedding). At inference time, the L2 norm of ∆embedding serves as a visual-variance indicator, which is compared with a threshold to determine whether early observation should be triggered.
Fig. 4: The Illustration of action saliency aware adaptive early observation:

Fig. 5: Real-world Setup. The real-world evaluation platform includes a tabletop workspace, a Franka Panda robotic manipulator fixed to the table, and a RGB camera for visual observation. All objects are positioned on the table surface.

Fig. 8: Screenshots of our video simulation results.The images in each row are spaced 4 seconds apart. Top two rows: open the top drawer and put the bowl inside. StreamingVLA completes the task in under 16 seconds, whereas pi0.5 has not yet picked up the bowl at the 20-second mark. Bottom two rows: put both the alphabet soup and the tomato sauce in the basket. At 20 seconds, StreamingVLA has already finished the pick-and-place operations for both objects, while pi0.5 is still on its way to picking up the second item.

Table 2: Ablation studies for StreamingVLA techniques validate the effectiveness of our Action Flow Matching(AFM) formulation w/o state-based alignment modeling(SA), normalization modification(NM) mentioned in Sec. 4.1, as well as adaptive early observation(EO) schemes. The “2” and “1.45” represents the average number of early observed actions. The "Random" represents employing early observation randomly.

Fig. 2: The overall methodology framework for StreamingVLA: We conduct a systematic timeline analysis and conclude the optimization target for fast and fluent execution. We present two key techniques: action flow matching and adaptive early observation, to overlap the latency of action execution with of action generation and VLM observation, respectively.
